title: Is Yoko Ono the Most Radical Artist of the Trump Era?
description: "In the 1960s, she invited an audience to cut off her clothes. As attacks on women’s rights escalate, 'Cut Piece' and other decades-old works of feminist art feel more relevant than ever."
hook: Yoko Ono's 1960s performance art feels prescient now—her body as political text in an era attacking women's autonomy.

title: The Man Who Reads Books For a Living (One Every Two Days)
description: "Clarke Seicher is something much more specific and much rarer: a professional book reader who evaluates literature specifically for screen adaptation."
hook: He reads two books a day for Hollywood studios, deciding which stories deserve screens. The invisible gatekeeper of adaptation.

title: Crossing the Wine Dark Sea by Emily Wilson review – a masterclass in translation
description:
The polarising translator of the Odyssey and the Iliad sets out her philosophy in this fascinating collection

Emily Wilson’s translations of the Odyssey in 2017 and the Iliad in 2023 are now the standard English-language versions, acclaimed for their conciseness and fluency. Her infatuation with Homer began at the age of eight, when her primary school put on a production of the Odyssey, with her in the role of Athena, and the excitement hasn’t worn off. You can question some of the choi
hook: Emily Wilson's radical translations of Homer rewrote how English speakers read ancient epics—here she explains her philosophy.

title: The Girl We Locked in the Trunk Is Very High Maintenance
description:
“Driving Through Pennsylvania” by Mack Gelber We’ve been driving through Pennsylvania for almost two months. People give us stunned looks when we tell them: “How long?” When they ask what brings us through, we tell them we’re traveling children’s entertainers, or we’re land surveyors scoping out sites for a new mall, or we’re searching for […]

The post The Girl We Locked in the Trunk
hook: A surreal road narrative about two people, a trunk, Pennsylvania, and the performance of normalcy that cracks under pressure.

title: For Ceija Stojka, Memory Is Survival
description: She rejected fascism not only by depicting what she endured in the Holocaust but also the tenderness of everyday Romani life.
hook: Ceija Stojka painted Romani life and Holocaust memory together—refusal to let fascism define what she could be.
